speaker: interviewer
text: A magic pill. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Faygeh, is there anything else you'd like to add? Something we didn't ask you? Something we might have left out? That you can think of?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I-- when I ended--

speaker: interviewer
text: We ended-- in-- --in Milwaukee, and your husband was making gloves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not making gloves. He cut gloves with a cutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: He cut gloves with a cutter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he-- he quit this job, because it was too hard, too-- too much. He went to Lake State Footwear Company, a shoe company. He worked over there, and there, he met a-- a German Jew, which he was in Shanghai during the war. And they started to peddle with a horse and wagon, peddle junk, paper bags. And then after that, they bought a-- a truck, and they pick up iron, old furnaces. He worked very, very, very hard to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And in-- we decided to-- to our-- our children should be educated Jewish and Hebrew, and that's all private. I have to-- I have to take in a lady-- 80 years old-- to take care of her and three meals a day. Special for education for our children, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you contact a Hasidic community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We belong to a congregation that Jehudah. Twersky, he's named. Up to date, he call me every Friday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he called me Tanta Faygeh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about-- about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a special person-- very unique and very educated. And he has a degree in-- in the-- you know, in-- psychology degree and in Hebrew, Talmudic. Very fine. Oh, there he is. Jack, can you bring the picture, you and Rabbi [? Michael. ?] Over there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So your children were educated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Educated, and my Shlomo has education as a rabbi. And he is-- he graduated as a PhD in sociology. My daughter is a social worker for-- four years in Hebrew education-- high school in Chicago. And then four years in UWM in-- in Milwaukee-- social worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now she teaches Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 grandchildren. Are they being raised given a Jewish education, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, day school, Hebrew day school. They teach Hebrew and English both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, is there anything that we've left out that you'd like to tell us about? Can you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my goal, and this is my pleasure. And that's why God let me to be alive-- to bring a-- a generation after. And this-- this is my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I pray to God for my child and grandchild and for everybody. I like to pray for everybody, not only for Jewish people, for everybody. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about the way you've lived your life? And what-- what continues to be important?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's important [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to have my husband and children, and grandchildren, and they should be healthy and happy. That's my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Faygeh. It's been a privilege to interview you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm Jack Porter. I have a very special relationship to my mother. I am her only, you know, living son who came immediately after the war-- born right in the middle of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, rather.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I just-- here, come a little closer to me. I just have to do this. Is that I was born December 2, 1944. And I've had a very special relationship with my mother my whole life. Her two sisters died. And I was born, you know-- that special child right after-- after they died. And ever since then, I've had this unbelievable close relationship to-- to Mama. And now I wanted to explain some of the people in these-- in this family. Right here is the Rabbi Twersky, Rabbitzen Twersky, Hasidic dynasty, very close to my mother and father. In the back here is my mother's second husband, Mr. Arenzon his--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stepson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stepson, right, the stepson. And well, maybe you can explain. And this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stepchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stepchildren from Arenzon. And here is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This one. This one. Show this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this one is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and-- and two-- two students. When my husband passed away, I-- I-- I-- I was very lucky, two girls-- I took in the first girl, Karen. She stayed with me one year, a student. Then the other, Margolis, another-- for-- for one year. Then I took one, Kelly, and she converted. She wanted to convert. She stayed for one year. Another one-- and the fourth one was a Russian girl, Jewish. And she stayed with me. She didn't have a place where to stay, and she stayed with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here is Mr. Arenzon, my mother's second husband and family.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his family and-- and our family. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And more of his family in the back. Here is Shlomo, my brother's daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oldest daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oldest daughter. And her name is-- you-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Malka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Malka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is a shot of my father. It's not the best shot. The other one is better. Right there, about what-- a year before he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he had cancer already. Held up by another survivor, Mr. [? Tuchman. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Two good friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two good friends. Mr. [? Tuchman. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Stein.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mr. Stein. Mr. [? Tuchman ?] was a survivor, who held him up and Mr. Stein, a good friend in Milwaukee. He kind of had a beard. He looked like Trotsky a little bit. Now, here is probably two of the most famous pictures. One is the partisan baby picture. This is-- Yes, this is Rabbi Twersky and the Rabbitzen Twersky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Congregation Beth Jehudah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Congregation Beth Jehudah in Milwaukee. A descendant of the Baal Shem Tov, one of the great Hasidic rabbis of our time. Very close friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mama, you still have a close relationship--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Rabbitzen.

speaker: interviewee
text: His son, [? Michael ?] Twersky, he calls me every Friday. And he calls me Tanta Faygh, How are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the time to zoom in, if you can, you know? Not that loose.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not that loose. How about like that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. A little looser. Pan right. A little looser and pan right. There, good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, this is Rabbi Twersky and Rabbitzen Twersky. He's-- he's a descendant of the Baal Shem Tov, one of the great Hasidic rabbis in Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] . Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife, the Rabbitzen Twersky, a very dear friend of my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very special lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very special.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very special.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his son, Rabbi [? Michael ?] Twersky-- well, you tell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: His son, Rabbi Michael Twersky-- he's a a very unique, very special person. He calls me every Friday. He calls me Tanta Faygeh. How are you? And he asks me for the children. And so shall I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, blessed memory, when we married off our daughter, Bella. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our family. My daughter and son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Show the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll hold it, Mom. You talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all her family.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Bella's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, uh-huh. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- let me take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Shlomo and his wife and his children.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] . All right. And then we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait. Let me get [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family. Mariam, Gabrielle, and Danielle.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father-in-law and mother-in-law of my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here. I'll hold it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yisroel. And this is my husband, me, and Jack, the little baby, in 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is-- these two photos are probably the most special.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me at the age of one. My father with his First Class Partisan medal, one of the few Jews to get it. And my mother, who was in the-- in the partisans as a cook and-- right after the war. And this is one of the few photos of my father's mother and father that I'm named after. You can see in the eyes the same Porter look.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- my oldest granddaughter, Esther Malka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, I'll hold it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? You want some more?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're trying to get her married.

speaker: interviewee
text: What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right here--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are-- these are--

speaker: interviewee
text: After my husband passed away, I took in students.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, let me just hold it for the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Karen and then Margolis and that's me. I have four or five students.

speaker: interviewee
text: That you took into your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: The last one was a Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a daughter to me. She was like a daughter to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother married a second man, Mr. Arenzon.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his son.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his son,

speaker: interviewee
text: Simon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Simon. And right here is his other son and wife. That's-- Mama. Mama, that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shelia and [? Moishe. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Shelia and [? Moishe, ?] [? Mauricio. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Mauricio. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I've been blessed with two great fathers, actually. My first one, Irving, and-- and then Ihuda there is my stepfather. Right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are pictures of-- in the forest, partisans, young children. You-- you explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah, these pictures-- some of them have come out of my-- my book on the partisans. These are pictures of young boys who fought-- many of them teenagers. Some of them came to Canada and to America. These little pom-poms show that they were orphans.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's more inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's many-- yeah, there's more. Some are in Israel, and some are in America, Canada. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Young photo-- young boys. Actually, I found that the pictures in my book are actually-- are clearer than that, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, much better. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] These were the two commanders, Nikolai [UKRANIAN] named [UKRANIAN] and Max [POLISH] Josef [POLISH] A Polish commander and a Ukrainian commander. Both non-Jews, but the fighting forces were all Jewish. The reason why-- it was good public relations to have a non-Jewish commander. These were two righteous Gentiles. They're Slovaks. The father and son, they gave my father a gun-- a rifle, 150 bullets. They saved many, many Jews. Two righteous Poles. And here are other fighters. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Here are more fighters, many with many more medals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fighters for the Fatherland First Class. War of the Fatherland, Partisan Medal, First Class. [NON-ENGLISH] Commander. Alexander [NON-ENGLISH] Commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have this in your book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have, but these are better pictures. Beryl [NON-ENGLISH] another leader. Women who were fighters. Rivka [NON-ENGLISH] Joseph [INAUDIBLE]. Most of these people are dead. [NON-ENGLISH] David [NON-ENGLISH]. And then pictures of the partisans marching. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Marching? This is [INAUDIBLE]. This is not marching.

speaker: interviewee
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [INAUDIBLE] the Nazis surround the forest in 1942, and we have to run out. Good people-- Ukraine people. They let us know, and we have to run out. In 1943, again, the Ukraine Nationalists came on one side. And the other side, the-- the Nazis came. And we have to move out of-- of this forest, complete. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, these are better.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are better pictures. These are rare pictures of the move out of the partisan. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Some of the soldiers were injured or in hospital. Some had tuberculosis from living in the woods. Some died even after the war from the life situations they had to live under. Tough guys and girls-- and women. And young boys-- teenagers. Boys and girls fought. Young partisans. Some 15 years old. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] They fought in an area-- I wanted to show the map here. If this is Warsaw up here, if we go to Radom, Lublin, Chelm. We cross over the border at Chelm, go into Kovel. Right there, Povors'k and Manevychi. That, where my finger is-- that's where the fighting took place in this area. And that's where the massacre was that my mother lost her family, Manevychi. That's in like a triangle. I was born here in Rivne, right there at the bottom of the triangle. And this is where my mother grew up and where they fought, and they died, and some lived in this area in the Western Ukraine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They bombed the city all year round. Every night, every night. The hospital. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Through.
